data.sentence: Hi, thanks for joining, this is Sarah Chen from Aegis Cloud Security support, I have Marcus Williams here with me as well — he handles a lot of our billing-side cases.
Hey there, Marcus Williams, good to be on.
Hi Sarah, hi Marcus, this is Kendra Wallace, I'm the CTO over at Brightpath Commerce — thanks for getting this scheduled quickly, I know it's end of month so I appreciate it.
Of course, yeah, end of month is always a busy time so we wanted to get eyes on this sooner rather than later for you.
So, just to frame this up — the case notes say you're seeing a discrepancy on your February invoice, is that right?
Yes, exactly. So we received the invoice on, I think it was February 20th, and the total came in about fourteen hundred dollars higher than what we were expecting based on our contract.
Okay, and the contract — is that the renewal you signed back in, uh, Q4 of last year?
Yeah, November. We renewed in November and locked in pricing for Aegis Protect and Aegis Detect, those are the two modules we're currently on.
Got it. And do you have the invoice number handy? I want to pull it up on my end while we're talking.
Yeah, one sec — it's INV-2026-0847.
INV-2026-0847, okay pulling that up now.

While Marcus is pulling that up, Kendra, can you tell me a little more about what specifically looked off — was it a line item that seemed wrong, or was it more like an unexpected charge category entirely?

So there's a line item that says, um, overage charges — data retention overage — and it's for something like 2.3 terabytes. And we just, we weren't expecting that at all. We've been pretty consistent in our data footprint, we're a retail operation, not a huge amount of log volume.
Okay yeah I see it, I've got the invoice open. The overage charge is listed under Aegis Protect, it's flagging 2.31 terabytes above your contracted tier.
Right, and that's what surprised us because we're on the, I believe it's the 10 terabyte tier, and we've never come close to that.
Yeah, so your contracted limit is 10 TB for Protect — Marcus, can you check what the usage reporting shows for January? That's the billing period this invoice covers.
Already on it, yeah. So... okay this is interesting. The usage report is showing 12.31 terabytes for the January period, but let me cross-check that against the actual backup logs.
And is that, um — is that 12 terabytes compressed or uncompressed? Because I know that can sometimes be the thing.
Good question, that's actually a really common source of confusion and it might be exactly what's happening here.

So, the billing metric we use for Protect is pre-compression — it's the raw data size before our dedup and compression runs. Some customers, especially if they changed any backup policies, can see that number jump even if the stored footprint looks the same on their end.

Hm. Okay, so that could be it. We did actually — our infrastructure team made some changes to our backup policy configuration in, I want to say early January. We added a few additional workloads to the backup scope.
That would do it, yeah. If you added workloads mid-cycle or even at the start of January, those would count toward the raw ingestion metric for the whole month.
Let me see if I can pull the backup policy change log for Brightpath's org. Give me just a moment.
Sure, no problem.

And Kendra, while he's doing that — does your team have visibility into the usage dashboard in the portal? Sometimes customers aren't aware that we have a real-time usage tracker under the Protect module settings.
Honestly I'm not sure if my team checks that regularly. I'm more on the strategy side, our infrastructure lead would be the one managing that day to day.

That's fair. It might be worth looping them in after this call — there's also an option to set usage threshold alerts so you get notified before you hit your limit, rather than finding out on the invoice.
Yeah that would actually be useful, we'd want that enabled going forward.

Okay, I've got the change log — so there's a policy update on January 3rd, looks like five new workloads were added to the Brightpath backup scope, and that includes what appears to be a couple of database instances that weren't in the previous config.

Yep, that tracks with what I was saying. So the overage is real in the sense that the usage did happen, but it sounds like it may have been an unintentional consequence of the policy change rather than something the team expected to trigger billing implications.

Right, and that's kind of my issue — like I'm not disputing that the data was backed up, I just don't think anyone on our team realized that adding those workloads would push us over the tier. Is there anything that can be done about the charge given that it was essentially, you know, a configuration thing and not like intentional overuse?

That's a fair point to raise. I don't have authority to adjust billing charges directly, that would need to go through your account team — but what I can do is document this in the case notes with the full context, the policy change date, the workloads added, so that when your account manager reviews it they have everything they need to make a determination.
Yeah and honestly that's the right path — billing adjustments or credits, those decisions sit with the account team, but a well-documented support case absolutely helps move that conversation along.
Okay, that works. Who's our account manager currently? I want to make sure I'm reaching out to the right person.
Let me look that up — it looks like your account manager is... Devon Park, and I can include their contact info in the follow-up email I send you after this call.
Perfect, yeah please include that. And just to confirm — you'll send me a summary of what we found today as well? The policy change, the usage breakdown, all of that?

Absolutely. I'll put together a case summary with the invoice reference, the January usage figures, the January 3rd policy change log entry, and a recommendation to connect with Devon about the billing review. You should have that within a couple hours.
Great. And just so I'm clear — nothing needs to change on our end right now in terms of the active configuration or the backup policy, right? Like this doesn't affect anything operationally.

Correct, no operational changes needed. Your backups are running fine, everything is healthy. This is purely a billing clarification. If you wanted to adjust your tier going forward you'd do that through your account manager, but there's nothing broken.
Okay good, that's good to hear. Alright, I think that covers everything I needed from this call. I appreciate you both digging into it.
Of course, happy to help work through it. Keep an eye out for that email, and feel free to reply directly if anything looks off or you have follow-up questions.
Thanks Kendra, good luck with the account team conversation and we'll get that case summary over to you shortly.
Thanks, take care.
